programs.name: Alabama Medicaid Elderly and Disabled Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs help staying home instead of moving to a nursing facility, this waiver covers personal care, homemaker services, and case management.

localEntities.name: Local AAAs (e.g., Area Agency on Aging of East Alabama, Aging South Alabama, Regional Planning Commission of Greater Birmingham)

programs.intro:
If your parent is at risk of nursing home placement but wants to stay home, Alabama's Elderly and Disabled Waiver provides the support services to make that possible. The waiver covers personal care, homemaker services, respite care, adult day health, companion services, home-delivered meals, and case management — all designed to help your parent remain safely at home.

Your parent must qualify for Medicaid and need nursing facility level of care (meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management). Income cannot exceed $2,829 per month and assets must stay below $2,000. Because enrollment is capped at 15,000 people annually, there's often a waitlist.

The program is administered through local Area Agencies on Aging across Alabama, so services and wait times may vary by region.

summary: Needs nursing facility level of care

exemptAssets: Primary home (if living there or intending to return)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing facility level of care — a clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management
otherRequirements: Disabled (as defined by Medicaid/SSI criteria) or elderly

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to apply — processing time varies and there's often a waitlist due to enrollment caps.

steps.title: Find your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Each region has its own AAA office that handles applications. Contact Alabama Department of Senior Services if you're unsure which office serves your area.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid if not already enrolled
steps.description: You must qualify for full Medicaid first. SSI recipients automatically meet the financial requirements.

steps.title: Get clinical assessment
steps.description: A physician must document that your parent needs nursing facility level of care — daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar activities.

steps.title: Submit waiver application
steps.description: Provide income proof, asset documentation, medical records, and residency verification to your local AAA office.

steps.title: Wait for slot availability
steps.description: If approved but no slots are available, your parent will be placed on a waitlist. A case manager will develop a care plan once enrollment begins.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Enrollment capped at 15,000 beneficiaries annually — waitlist likely if slots are full
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if there's a waitlist. Your parent's place in line is secured, and needs can change quickly.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward the $2,000 Limit
included: Life insurance with cash value over $1,500
excluded: Primary home (if your parent lives there or intends to return)
Burial plots and prepaid funeral expenses up to $1,500

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Alabama residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Financial
items: SSI award letters or pay stubs

categories.name: Medical
items: Physician statement documenting need for nursing facility level of care
Recent medical records showing functional limitations

contentSections.text: Alabama has a 60-month Look-Back Rule. If your parent gave away assets for less than fair market value in the past 5 years, it will create a penalty period that delays eligibility. This includes gifts to family members.
Your parent's home is protected as long as they live there or intend to return, but Alabama can recover costs from the estate after death. Plan accordingly with an elder law attorney.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for the waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt as long as she lives there or intends to return. However, Alabama may recover waiver costs from her estate after death through Estate Recovery, so consult an elder law attorney about protection strategies.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the $2,829 limit?
faqs.answer: She won't qualify. Unlike some programs, there's no spend-down option for the waiver. However, if her income changes (like a reduction in benefits), she can reapply.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist if there are no slots available?
faqs.answer: Alabama doesn't publish waitlist times, but with only 15,000 slots statewide, waits can be significant. Apply anyway to secure her place in line, and contact your local Area Agency on Aging for regional estimates.

faqs.question: What happens if she gives money to grandchildren before applying?
faqs.answer: Any gifts in the past 60 months will create a penalty period that delays eligibility. The penalty is calculated by dividing the gift amount by Alabama's average nursing home cost. Don't transfer assets without consulting an elder law attorney first.

faqs.question: Does she need to be on regular Medicaid first?
faqs.answer: Yes, she must qualify for full Medicaid before applying for the waiver. SSI recipients automatically meet the financial requirements. If she's not on Medicaid, start that application first through your local Department of Human Resources office.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this waiver and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Regular Medicaid primarily covers medical care. This waiver adds home and community-based services like personal care, homemaker services, and respite care — specifically to help people avoid nursing home placement. You need both regular Medicaid and waiver approval.

programs.name: Alabama PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical services, transportation, and daily care under one program.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Alabama PACE may let them stay at home while receiving comprehensive medical care, transportation, meals, and personal care services. Because PACE covers all medical care under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, specialists, or home care agencies — one team manages everything from primary care to prescription drugs to adult day care.

PACE is currently only available in Mobile and Baldwin counties through Mercy LIFE of Alabama, with enrollment capped at 185 participants. Your parent must qualify for nursing facility level of care (the same assessment used to determine if someone needs nursing home care) but be able to live safely at home with PACE support. There are no strict income limits to enroll, but Medicaid eligibility is needed for free coverage — otherwise your parent pays a monthly premium for private coverage.

summary: Nursing facility level of care needed
Live in Mobile or Baldwin County
Able to live safely at home with support
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing facility level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities due to physical or cognitive impairment)
otherRequirements: Must reside in PACE service area (Mobile or Baldwin counties)
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE services

applicationGuide.summary: Call Mercy LIFE of Alabama at 251-287-8427 to start the application and nursing facility level of care assessment.

steps.title: Call Mercy LIFE of Alabama
steps.description: Contact 251-287-8427 to discuss your parent's needs and confirm they live in the Mobile or Baldwin County service area.

steps.title: Schedule assessment
steps.description: A PACE team will schedule a comprehensive assessment to determine if your parent meets nursing facility level of care requirements.

steps.title: Complete Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: If seeking free coverage, complete Alabama Medicaid long-term care eligibility assessment for income and assets.

steps.title: Wait for enrollment
steps.description: Due to enrollment cap of 185 participants, you may be placed on a waitlist if the program is full.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Enrollment capped at 185 per program; potential waitlist due to capacity
applicationGuide.tip: Call even if your parent's income seems too high — private pay options are available without Medicaid eligibility.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Free Coverage (Medicaid)
contentSections.footnote: 2025 Alabama Medicaid long-term care limits (300% Federal Benefit Rate). Private pay available above these limits.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Free Coverage

categories.name: Financial
items: Medicare and Medicaid cards if applicable

categories.name: Medical
items: Assessment records from other care providers

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in Mobile and Baldwin counties. Enrollment is capped at 185 participants, so there may be a waitlist even if your parent qualifies.
Your parent must be certified as needing nursing facility level of care but still able to live safely at home with PACE support. This is different from needing immediate nursing home placement.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is slightly above $2,901 per month?
faqs.answer: She can still enroll in PACE through private pay with a monthly premium. The income limit only applies to getting free coverage through Medicaid. Contact Mercy LIFE at 251-287-8427 for private pay rates.

faqs.question: Can Dad keep his house if he qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from asset limits regardless of value. He can keep his house and still qualify for free PACE coverage through Medicaid if his other assets are under $2,000.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom needs nursing home care while in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care when needed as part of the comprehensive benefit. The PACE team coordinates all care transitions and there are no additional costs for temporary or permanent nursing facility stays.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover prescription medications?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE covers all prescription drugs as part of the comprehensive benefit with no deductibles or copays for enrollees. The PACE pharmacy team manages all medications.

faqs.question: What if we live in Birmingham or Huntsville?
faqs.answer: PACE is currently only available in Mobile and Baldwin counties. You would need to explore other Alabama Medicaid waiver programs or private care options in your area.

faqs.question: How long does the nursing facility level of care assessment take?
faqs.answer: The assessment timeline isn't specified, but it involves a comprehensive evaluation by the PACE team. Call 251-287-8427 to get a realistic timeline for your parent's situation and current enrollment capacity.

programs.name: Alabama Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent struggles with Medicare premiums and medical bills, these programs can save them $2,200+ annually on Medicare costs.
programs.intro:
If your parent's monthly income is below $1,850 and they're enrolled in Medicare, Alabama's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate their Medicare Part B premium ($185/month in 2026) and potentially cover all Medicare deductibles and copays. These three programs — QMB, SLMB, and QI — are income-tiered, with the lowest-income seniors receiving the most comprehensive coverage.

Because QMB covers all Medicare cost-sharing, your parent cannot be billed by doctors or hospitals for Medicare-covered services — a protection worth thousands annually for someone with frequent medical needs. QMB recipients also automatically qualify for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, capping most medications at $12.65 per prescription.

The QI program has limited federal funding and operates first-come, first-served, so applying early in the year increases approval chances.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premium savings ($2,220/year) plus Medicare deductibles and copays for QMB recipients

exemptAssets: Burial plots and funds up to $1,500 per person
Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

otherRequirements: Enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B
Cannot receive full Medicaid (QI program only)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at medicaid.alabama.gov or call 1-800-362-1504 — processing takes 1-3 months depending on program tier.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security statements, pension records), bank statements, Medicare card, and identification.

steps.title: Complete Medicaid application
steps.description: Apply online at medicaid.alabama.gov using the MEDICAID application portal, or download Form MG-14-MA to mail.

steps.title: Submit supporting documents
steps.description: Upload documents online or mail to your local Medicaid district office with your completed application.

steps.title: Follow up on processing
steps.description: QMB coverage begins the month after approval. SLMB and QI coverage can be retroactive up to 3 months if eligible.

applicationGuide.processingTime: QMB: Active month after approval; SLMB/QI: Active month of application, retroactive up to 3 months prior if eligible
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI only: Limited funding; first-come, first-served, priority to prior recipients; may close when funds exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the calendar year before federal funding runs out

contentSections.heading: 2026 Income Limits by Program Tier
contentSections.footnote: 2026 federal limits; adds approximately $500/month per additional household member

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $9,950 (Individual) / $14,910 (Couple)
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500 face value
excluded: Primary home and property it sits on
Burial plots and burial funds up to $1,500 per person
Life insurance with face value $1,500 or less

tiers.name: QMB
tiers.description: Lowest income tier with most comprehensive coverage
tiers.coverage: Part A premium (if applicable), Part B premium ($185/month), all Medicare deductibles and copays, automatic Extra Help for prescriptions

tiers.name: QI
tiers.description: Highest income tier with limited funding
tiers.coverage: Part B premium only ($185/month) — no Medicaid card issued

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Birth certificate or other age verification

contentSections.text: QI program funding is limited nationwide. Apply early in the year before federal funds are exhausted. Previous recipients get priority, but new applicants are served first-come, first-served.
If your parent has QMB coverage and receives a bill for Medicare-covered services, report it immediately. Providers cannot legally charge QMB recipients for Medicare deductibles or copays.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home and the land it sits on are completely exempt from asset limits, regardless of value. Only countable assets like bank accounts and investments matter for the $9,950 individual limit.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: She can still qualify for SLMB, which covers the $185/month Medicare Part B premium. While SLMB doesn't cover deductibles and copays like QMB does, it still saves $2,220 annually on premiums.

faqs.question: Does Dad need to drop his Medicare Supplement plan if he gets QMB?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily, but QMB may make it unnecessary. QMB covers all Medicare deductibles and copays that supplement plans typically cover. Review his specific supplement benefits to see if the overlap makes the premium worth paying.

faqs.question: How long does approval take, and when does coverage start?
faqs.answer: QMB coverage begins the month after approval. SLMB and QI coverage can start the month you apply and may be retroactive up to 3 months if you were eligible during that time.

faqs.question: What happens if the QI program runs out of funding?
faqs.answer: New QI applications stop being accepted until the next federal fiscal year (October). Previous QI recipients get priority for renewal. This is why applying early in the year is critical for QI.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both Medicare Savings Program and SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, these programs don't conflict. Your parent can receive QMB, SLMB, or QI coverage while also getting SNAP food benefits, as long as they meet the income requirements for each program separately.
